heading: SAGGIO INTRODUTTIVO LXXXVII
card_summary: This chunk from Campanini's introduction to De Harmonia Mundi examines Zorzi's categorization of Jewish interpreters into Talmudists and Kabbalists, then provides a detailed philological survey of the five "Talmudist" authorities Zorzi names: Rashi (Rabbi Salomon), David Qimchi, Levi ben Gershom (Ralbag/Gersonides), the enigmatic "Rebunachi" (possibly Mosheh Qimchi or Rabbi Achai), and Maimonides (cited negatively alongside Aristotle and Averroes). Campanini also highlights a notable first citation in Western literature: Zorzi's reference to Shem Tov ibn Falaqera, traced to the Sefer ha-ma'alot, citing a pseudo-Aristotelian passage ultimately derived from Plotinus's Enneads via the Theologia Aristotelis. The chunk also notes Zorzi's dependence on Reuchlin's De arte cabalistica for his conceptual framework of Jewish exegetical schools.
essay:
## Overview

Pages 87–91 of Campanini's introductory essay present a close philological and intellectual-historical analysis of the Jewish authorities cited in *De Harmonia Mundi*, focusing on the group Zorzi classifies as "Talmudists" — exegetes who attend to the literal, active, and legal dimensions of Scripture rather than the contemplative-mystical path of the Kabbalists. This section bridges Zorzi's abstract distinction between the two schools and the concrete bibliography he deploys.

## The Talmudist / Kabbalist Distinction

Campanini opens by restating Zorzi's fundamental hermeneutical polarity. The Talmudist orients all speculation toward "questo mondo inferiore" — the lower, sensible world — and is characterized by servile fear of God through meticulous observance of the commandments. The Kabbalist, by contrast, practices "filial love" (*amore filiale*) and devotes himself primarily to contemplation, reading all Scripture anagogically, numerologically, or through *symbolica ratio*. The two schools borrow from each other, but the orientation differs fundamentally.

Zorzi's own words, quoted directly by Campanini, articulate the threefold (or fourfold) hermeneutical method of the Kabbalists:

> Altri, che preferiscono lasciare ai Talmudisti la cura per le realtà mondane, le faccende giuridiche e qualunque pratica [usus] di diritto pubblico e privato, rendendosi disponibili per la contemplazione e per la vita superiore, riferiscono l'intero contenuto delle scritture all'Archetipo: a Lui rinviano tutte le loro interpretazioni, ricavate mediante il ricorso ai numeri ovvero seguendo la logica dei simboli [symbolica ratio] o, infine, attenendosi al significato anagogico.

<cite>p. 87–88</cite>

This passage is significant for Zorzi's cosmological project: the Archetype (*Archetipo*) to which all Kabbalist interpretation returns is the highest principle of his harmonic universe, the divine source whose harmony is refracted through all levels of creation. The three methods — numerical, symbolic (*symbolica ratio*), and anagogical — correspond to the three classic Kabbalist hermeneutical techniques (gematria, allegory, and mystical ascent).

Campanini notes that this passage was translated into French in the seventeenth century by Claude Duret, who expanded it by introducing a third category — Jewish philosophers — not present in Zorzi's original binary schema.

## Dependence on Reuchlin

A key editorial observation is Zorzi's clear debt to Johannes Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica* for the conceptual framework organizing Jewish literature into these schools. However, Campanini stresses that Zorzi's actual citations of the named authors in *De Harmonia Mundi* are largely independent of Reuchlin and reflect some direct, if partial, familiarity with the texts.

## The Five Talmudist Authorities

### 1. Rabbi Salomon (Rashi)

Zorzi refers to Rabbi Shelomoh ben Yitzchaq of Troyes (Champagne) — Rashi — following the standard Christian convention. Rashi's biblical commentary enjoyed a diffusion comparable to the *glossa ordinaria* of the Latin Bible. Zorzi cites Rashi explicitly on the figure of Melchisedec (Gen. 14:18), arguing that "Melchisedec" is an appellative (*nomen appellativum*) meaning "just king," while the proper name is Shem, son of Noah — a reading also supported by Jerome:

> Tuttavia rabbi Salomon, e con lui molti sapienti ebrei, con i quali concorda Gerolamo, afferma che Melchisedec è un attributo [nomen appellativum] e significa 're giusto', mentre il nome proprio è Sem: si trattava cioè del figlio di Noè.

<cite>p. 88</cite>

Campanini notes the irony that Jerome, who preceded Rashi by centuries, is cited in support of the rabbi, because Rashi himself relied on a *midrash aggadah* that preserves ancient tradition.

### 2. David Qimchi

David ben Yosef Qimchi (referred to as "David Chimchi"), member of a distinguished dynasty of grammarians and exegetes from Provence, is cited at least once in *De Harmonia Mundi*, apparently from his Torah commentary or the *Sefer ha-shorashim* (his Hebrew lexicon).

### 3. Levi ben Gershom (Gersonides / Ralbag)

Under the slightly misleading form "Levi Bengarson," Zorzi refers to the philosopher-exegete Levi ben Gershom, known by the acronym Ralbag and in Christian circles as Gersonides or Leone di Bagnols (from Bagnols-sur-Cèze in Languedoc). Campanini observes that no explicit quotation from Ralbag's works appears in *De Harmonia Mundi*, which makes it impossible to assess the depth of Zorzi's knowledge. Ralbag does appear in Reuchlin's *De arte cabalistica* as "Rabbi Levi Ben Gersom" / "Magister Leo de Banolis," but Campanini judges direct dependence on Reuchlin implausible here.

### 4. "Rebunachi" — A Crux

This designation resists identification and has no parallel in standard repertories of Hebrew literature. Campanini proposes two hypotheses: (a) Zorzi may have misread frequent references in David Qimchi's commentary to his brother Mosheh Qimchi, which are routinely introduced by the formula *Rabbi Achi* ("my master, my brother"); (b) alternatively, Zorzi may have contracted a reference to *Rabbi Achai* (in Aramaic), an authority frequently cited in Talmudic literature, or to Acha of Shava, the Geonic halakhic author of the *She'iltot* (legal sermons).

### 5. Maimonides (Moses filius Maimoni / Rambam)

Maimonides is classified among the Talmudists, presumably because of his halakhic work and Aristotelian commitments. In *De Harmonia Mundi* he appears three times: first, negatively associated with Aristotle and Averroes; second, as "Mosè di Cordova" alongside Galen on the ancient principle of punishing a wrongdoer in the organ used to commit the crime; and third, via his most famous work, the *Guide for the Perplexed*, cited under the title *Liber de directione*.

## Shem Tov ibn Falaqera — A Significant Discovery

Campanini reserves particular attention for a Jewish philosopher Zorzi cites but does not include in his Talmudist list: Shem Tov ben Joseph ibn Falaqera, rendered as "Rabbi Semtoph ben Falchiera." The Latin citation reads:

> Meminit etiam Rabi Semtoph ben Falchiera Aristotelem testari in [libro de Bdello quod anima sua, resoluta a sensibus, elata fuit ad contemplationem sublimium].

<cite>p. 90</cite>

Campanini identifies this as, to his knowledge, the first citation of Ibn Falaqera in Western literature. The source is traced to Ibn Falaqera's *Sefer ha-ma'alot* ("Book of Degrees"), where a passage of clear Neoplatonic character is attributed to Aristotle. The puzzling title "Sullo bdellio" (*de Bdello*) does not correspond to any known Aristotelian work. Campanini's analysis traces the passage's genealogy: it derives from the pseudo-Aristotelian *Theologia Aristotelis*, which attributes to Aristotle excerpts from Plotinus's *Enneads* translated from Arabic. This work was first published in Latin translation in Rome in 1519. Ibn Falaqera, however, cited it directly from Arabic — as evidenced by the fact that his quotation in *Sefer ha-ma'alot* is more extensive than the version in the *Ikhwan al-safa* encyclopaedia of the "Brethren of Purity," suggesting direct access to the Arabic pseudo-Aristotelian text rather than mediation through the encyclopaedic tradition.

## Significance for Zorzi's Project

This section reveals the breadth and limits of Zorzi's Jewish learning. His framework is substantially Reuchlinian, but his specific citations — Rashi on Melchisedec, Qimchi's lexicography, and the remarkable Falaqera passage linking Aristotle to Plotinian contemplation — show independent engagement with Hebrew sources. The Falaqera citation is particularly revealing: it integrates a Neoplatonic motif (the soul's liberation from the senses and ascent to contemplation) into *De Harmonia Mundi*'s overarching project of demonstrating that all wisdom traditions — Jewish, Greek, and Christian — harmonize in a single cosmic order whose source is the divine Archetype.
